Party_Details.Name: Kwee Lee Fung Ivon

Facts.Text: In 1988, Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd was incorporated and issued two shares, one each to Dr Lim and Dr Kwee.
At all material times, Dr Lim, Dr Kwee and Dr Lim’s mother, Mdm Irene Goh, were the company’s directors.
On 21 January 2010, Dr Kwee commenced divorce proceedings against Dr Lim.

In July 2010, Dr Lim incorporated another company, named 'Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd' ('the rival company'). He is the sole shareholder of the rival company, which, like the company, uses the Gleneagles property as its registered address.
From 1 October 2010 onwards, the rival company took over the company’s Gleneagles property as its place of business and paid the company a monthly rental of $8,000 for the use of the said premises.
Dr Lim ceased to work under the company’s banner in order to helm the rival company’s medical practice.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a shareholder-director can obtain leave to bring a derivative action on behalf of a company when another equal shareholder-director establishes a competing business at the same premises.
Party_Details.Rule: Under company law, leave to bring a derivative action requires the complainant to act in good faith and demonstrate that the action is prima facie in the company's interests. A director who establishes a competing business using the company's premises must properly manage conflicts of interest and make appropriate disclosures.
Party_Details.Application: Two doctors each held half of the shares in a clinic and served as co-directors. The company operated a profitable medical practice with revenues exceeding $1 million. Following the initiation of divorce proceedings, one doctor incorporated a new, wholly-owned corporate entity. The new entity took over the clinic's premises, paid a fixed monthly rental to the original company, and absorbed the medical practice. This shift significantly reduced the original company's revenue, forming the basis for a shareholder dispute regarding diverted corporate opportunities.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A shareholder's financial interest in recovering a company's lost profits and improving the value of their shares aligns with the company's inherent commercial interests, satisfying the statutory requirement of good faith. The establishment of a rival clinic at the company's premises by a co-director without formal board disclosure establishes a legitimate and arguable prima facie case of breached fiduciary duties. Consequently, the statutory thresholds were met, and leave was granted to commence a derivative action on behalf of the company to recover the diverted profits.

Party_Details.Name: Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1988 to operate a medical practice, with equal shares held by Dr Lim and Dr Kwee.
The company operated a highly profitable medical practice at the Gleneagles property, generating revenues exceeding $1,000,000 in both 2008 and 2009.
For the first nine months of 2010, the company generated $1,102,019 in revenue.
From 1 October 2010, the company's registered premises were taken over by a newly incorporated rival entity.

After its premises and medical practice were absorbed by the rival entity, the company was converted into a landlord collecting a flat $8,000 monthly rent, causing its revenue for the last quarter of 2010 to plummet to $24,000.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the shift of medical practice operations from a company to a rival entity at the same premises justifies a derivative action being brought in the company's name.
Party_Details.Rule: For a derivative action to proceed, it must appear that the company has an arguable claim arising from conduct that damaged its business or financial position, and that pursuing the claim is in the company’s commercial interests.
Party_Details.Application: A company operated a highly profitable medical practice until a new corporate entity, controlled by one of the directors, took over its physical premises. The original company ceased its medical operations and was functionally converted into a landlord collecting a fixed monthly rent from the new entity. Following this transition, the original company's quarterly revenue dropped drastically. This severe structural shift forms the factual basis for assessing whether the company suffered actionable harm.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A derivative action is legally justified where there is a legitimate and arguable claim that a director breached fiduciary duties by diverting the core business to a rival entity. It is inherently in the company's practical and commercial interests to pursue the recovery of lost profits, especially when the offending director possesses the financial capacity to satisfy a judgment. Therefore, leave was granted for the derivative action to proceed in the company's name, with the company ordered to bear the costs of the application on an indemnity basis.

Party_Details.Name: Gordon Lim Boon Hui

Facts.Text: In 1988, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui became a shareholder of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd with one issued share.
Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui was a director of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd.
At all material times, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui practised at Gordon Lim Clinic for Women operated by Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd at the Gleneagles property.
On 21 January 2010, divorce proceedings between Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui and Dr Ivon Kwee Lee Fung were commenced.
In July 2010, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui incorporated Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd, with himself as the sole shareholder, using the Gleneagles property as its registered address.

From 1 October 2010 onwards, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui stopped working under Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd’s banner and led the medical practice of Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd at the Gleneagles property.
From 1 October 2010 onwards, the rival company took over the company’s Gleneagles property as its place of business and paid the company a monthly rental of $8,000 for the use of the said premises.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director breaches fiduciary duties by establishing and operating a competing medical company at the original company's premises and diverting its core business.
Party_Details.Rule: A director who controls or participates in another business that uses the company’s premises or overlaps with the company’s business must strictly manage any resulting conflict of interest, making full disclosures and ensuring transactions are approved through proper corporate channels.
Party_Details.Application: A director and 50% shareholder of a profitable clinic incorporated a new corporate entity, wholly owned by himself, using the exact same registered address. He subsequently transferred the medical practice to the new entity and arranged for the new entity to pay a fixed monthly rental to the original company. This unilateral restructuring removed the medical revenues from the original company and replaced them with a flat rental income, without securing independent board approval.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A professional practicing through a corporate vehicle and serving as its director remains strictly bound by fiduciary obligations to that company. Setting up a rival clinic at the same premises, absorbing the original company's medical practice, and converting the original company into a mere landlord without formal, independent board disclosure constitutes a prima facie breach of fiduciary duties. This self-interested restructuring establishes an arguable case of diverted corporate opportunities warranting a full hearing. Leave was granted to bring the derivative action against the director.

Party_Details.Name: Mdm Irene Goh

Facts.Text: Mdm Irene Goh, the mother of Dr Lim, served as a director of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd from 1988 onwards alongside Dr Lim and Dr Kwee.
Following the incorporation of the rival entity, Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd, Mdm Irene Goh also served as a director of that new rival company.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director closely connected to a conflicted co-director is permitted to have conduct of derivative proceedings brought on behalf of the company.
Party_Details.Rule: To protect the integrity of a derivative action, control of the litigation must be vested in a party free from conflicts of interest regarding the underlying breach of fiduciary duty.
Party_Details.Application: A director served on the board of the original clinic alongside her son and her daughter-in-law. When her son established a rival clinic at the same premises to divert the business, she also served as a director of that new rival entity. Her dual directorships and familial relationship placed her at the center of the governance surrounding the disputed business transfer.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A director who is an immediate family member of the target of a derivative action, and who simultaneously serves as a director of the rival entity that improperly absorbed the company's business, possesses an incurable conflict of interest. Consequently, this director is legally disqualified from having conduct of the derivative proceedings on behalf of the original company. Control of the litigation must be granted to the unconflicted shareholder.
